content.problemStatement:
Pep is known for his golden bids on football matches. Surprisingly, he has a rather simple strategy!

Team AAA and team PPP are playing against each other, and each of them have NNN players.

For each iii from 111 to NNN,


Pep believes that the iii-th player on team AAA has an attack power of attAiattA_iattAi​, and a defense power of defAidefA_idefAi​.
Similarly, the iii-th player on team PPP has an attack power of attPiattP_iattPi​, and a defense power of defPidefP_idefPi​.
The total attack power of a team is the sum of the attack powers of all of its NNN players.

The total defense power is defined similarly.

If the total attack power of one team is **strictly** greater than the total attack power of the other team, and its total defense power is **strictly** greater than the total defense power of the other team, Pep bids for that team.

Otherwise, he'll bid for a draw.

Can you tell what Pep will bid on?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of five lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN, the number of players on both teams.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers attA1,attA2,…,attANattA_1, attA_2, \ldots, attA_NattA1​,attA2​,…,attAN​ — the elements of attAattAattA.
The third line contains NNN space-separated integers defA1,defA2,…,defANdefA_1, defA_2, \ldots, defA_NdefA1​,defA2​,…,defAN​ — the elements of defAdefAdefA.
The fourth line contains NNN space-separated integers attP1,attP2,…,attPNattP_1, attP_2, \ldots, attP_NattP1​,attP2​,…,attPN​ — the elements of attPattPattP.
The fifth line contains NNN space-separated integers defP1,defP2,…,defPNdefP_1, defP_2, \ldots, defP_NdefP1​,defP2​,…,defPN​ — the elements of defPdefPdefP.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line a string representing what Pep bids for: either `"A"`, `"P"`, or `"DRAW"` (without quotes).


Each letter of the output may be printed in either lowercase or uppercase, i.e, the strings `DRAW`, `draw`, `dRaW`, and so on will be considered equivalent.
constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 2000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains integer N, the number of players in each team. The second line contains N integers attA1 … attAN – attack powers of team AAA. The third line contains N integers defA1 … defAN – defense powers of team AAA. The fourth line contains N integers attP1 … attPN – attack powers of team PPP. The fifth line contains N integers defP1 … defPN – defense powers of team PPP.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "A" if team AAA strictly dominates both total attack and total defense, "P" if team PPP strictly dominates both, otherwise output "DRAW".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: AAA total attack = 36, defense = 9; PPP total attack = 31, defense = 8. AAA strictly dominates, so output A.\nTest case 2: AAA attack = 101, defense = 10; PPP attack = 0, defense = 10. Attack dominates but defense does not (not strictly greater), so DRAW.\nTest case 3: AAA attack = 1, defense = 2; PPP attack = 3, defense = 4. PPP dominates both, so output P.
Both teams have equal attack and defense (0), no strict dominance, therefore DRAW.
Attacks are equal (10 each). Even though AAA defense (12) > PPP defense (10), attack is not strictly greater, so result is DRAW.

hidden.label: edge case – equal attack, AAA higher defense
general case – AAA better attack only

solution.approach: For each test case compute four sums: totalAttackA, totalDefenseA, totalAttackP, totalDefenseP by iterating over the N players once. Compare the two teams: if totalAttackA > totalAttackP AND totalDefenseA > totalDefenseP, answer is "A"; else if totalAttackP > totalAttackA AND totalDefenseP > totalDefenseA, answer is "P"; otherwise answer is "DRAW". This runs in O(N) time per test case and O(1) extra space.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) per test case, overall O(sum of N) ≤ O(2000).
